topic: Natural Language Processing: Transformers, Attention, and Tokenization

questions.scenario: An enterprise healthcare platform deploys a pre-trained BERT model utilizing standard WordPiece tokenization (trained on general corpora like Wikipedia) to extract diagnostic information from Electronic Health Records (EHR). During production, the system experiences severe memory spikes and latency degradation when processing complex clinical notes containing heavy medical terminology (e.g., 'subarachnoid', 'hypercholesterolemia'). Profiling reveals that single medical words are being hyper-fragmented into 10–15 subword tokens, hitting sequence length limits (L=512) and exponentially increasing attention computation.
questions.question: Which strategy best addresses both the sequence length inflation and processing latency without changing the base model architecture?
options: A) Adapt the vocabulary using domain-specific tokenization (e.g., BioBERT/ClinicalBERT vocabularies) and perform vocabulary adaptation/fine-tuning.
B) Replace WordPiece with Byte-Level Byte-Pair Encoding (BBPE) and strictly truncate input sequences to the first 256 subwords.
C) Increase the maximum sequence length limit to 2048 tokens and enable multi-GPU parallel processing to handle quadratic complexity.
D) Re-tokenize the text using character-level tokenization to eliminate Out-Of-Vocabulary (OOV) tokens entirely.
questions.correct_answer_text: A) Adapt the vocabulary using domain-specific tokenization (e.g., BioBERT/ClinicalBERT vocabularies) and perform vocabulary adaptation/fine-tuning.
questions.explanation: Standard subword tokenizers trained on general text over-fragment rare domain-specific terms into excessive subwords. This inflates sequence length L, leading to O(L^2) attention overhead and contextual fragmentation. Adapting the vocabulary with a domain-specific tokenizer merges specialized medical terms back into single/few tokens, significantly shortening sequence length, reducing attention computation, and improving semantic preservation.

questions.scenario: A financial media group runs a real-time event extraction engine processing continuous streams of long-form quarterly reports (averaging 3,000+ tokens per report). The latency target is sub-100ms per batch. The team uses standard Multi-Head Self-Attention, but runtime performance scales quadratically with document length (O(N^2)), causing frequent SLA breaches.
questions.question: To achieve sub-100ms latency on long sequences while maintaining global contextual dependencies across the document, which architectural shift represents the optimal performance-complexity trade-off?
options: A) Implement a sparse or linear attention mechanism (such as Longformer's sliding window with global attention or Linformer) to lower complexity to O(N).
B) Truncate all documents to 512 tokens using standard dot-product attention to guarantee fixed latency.
C) Replace the self-attention layer with a Bidirectional LSTM layer featuring additive attention to eliminate the sequence length bottleneck.
D) Double the number of attention heads while halving the per-head projection dimension (d_k) to maintain constant total parameters.
questions.correct_answer_text: A) Implement a sparse or linear attention mechanism (such as Longformer's sliding window with global attention or Linformer) to lower complexity to O(N).
questions.explanation: Standard Scaled Dot-Product Attention has quadratic O(N^2) time and memory complexity relative to sequence length N. Linear/Sparse attention models like Longformer combine local windowed attention with targeted global attention, dropping complexity to O(N). This permits the processing of full 3,000+ token documents within tight real-time latency budgets without resorting to information loss via severe truncation.

questions.scenario: An AI engineering team deploys a 7-billion parameter auto-regressive Decoder-only Transformer model for automated customer support chat. During token-by-token generation, latency increases linearly with each generated token, severely lowering throughput. Profiling shows that at step 't', the model redundant-computes Key (K) and Value (V) projections for all preceding tokens (1 to t-1).
questions.question: Which inference optimization technique directly eliminates this redundant tensor computation across auto-regressive generation steps?
options: A) Implement Key-Value (KV) Caching with dynamic memory management like PagedAttention.
B) Convert the Decoder dynamic attention mask into a static bidirectional attention mask.
C) Apply INT8 dynamic weight quantization exclusively to the Query (Q) projection matrices.
D) Replace standard scaled dot-product attention with FlashAttention during the prefill phase only.
questions.correct_answer_text: A) Implement Key-Value (KV) Caching with dynamic memory management like PagedAttention.
questions.explanation: In auto-regressive generation, computing token 't' requires Keys and Values of all previous tokens. Without KV Caching, K and V matrices for tokens 1..t-1 are recomputed at every step, creating O(T^2) cumulative work. KV Caching stores pre-computed K and V tensors in memory so that only K_t and V_t are calculated at step t. Coupled with PagedAttention, memory fragmentation is minimized, resolving the throughput bottleneck.
